nativeRange.habitat: Found in recesses, caves, and overhangs of deep coral reefs, typically at depths of 15–70 m. Described as secretive; generally observed hovering near cave entrances rather than swimming in open water.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting meaty preparations in captivity, including mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and quality frozen carnivore preparations. New arrivals may initially accept only live brine shrimp; a gradual transition to frozen mysis or copepods is generally advised. Tends to ambush small prey from shelter rather than chase food in open water.

goodWith: most reef invertebrates larger than its mouth
avoidWith: very small ornamental shrimp and crustaceans
tiny gobies and other bite-sized fish
boisterous tankmates that outcompete for food

summary: A tiny, strikingly-patterned Caribbean basslet (Liopropoma carmabi) with alternating yellow-orange and lavender stripes edged in red. Generally considered reef-safe; tends to inhabit caves and overhangs in deeper reef zones.
careNotes:
The Deepwater Candy Basslet is a small, secretive cave-dweller from the deeper zones of Western Atlantic reefs (15–70 m), where it is rarely seen in the open. In the aquarium it spends most of its time hovering near a favored cave entrance or tucked into rockwork crevices; a mature aquascape with deep caves, overhangs, and shaded recesses is generally advised so the fish feels secure enough to venture out and feed regularly.

Generally considered reef-safe, though it has been known to consume very small ornamental crustaceans and bite-sized fish that fit within its mouth; larger shrimp (e.g., cleaner shrimp) and corals tend to be left alone. Tends to be peaceful with unrelated tankmates but is reported to show aggression toward conspecifics — best kept singly unless sourcing a confirmed mated pair.

Because it originates from deeper water, it is generally advised to provide lower-intensity aquarium lighting or shadier areas where it can retreat. New arrivals may initially accept only live brine shrimp and require a patient transition to frozen foods; targeted feeding near its cave entrance is commonly recommended.

Cross-source notes: temperature is the most disagreed-upon parameter. LiveAquaria lists 68–74°F (20–23°C), which appears atypically cold for a tropical Caribbean species; FishBase reports wild temperatures of 24.2–28°C (mean 26.8°C) for the natural range, while Marine Collectors and the wider hobby consensus suggest 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C). The averaged range of 22–26°C is used here; some aquarists have noted that slightly cooler temperatures (closer to 22–24°C) may reflect the fish's deepwater origin and reduce stress. Temperament is listed as peaceful by LiveAquaria but semi-aggressive by Marine Collectors and the broader hobby; semi-aggressive is used here as the more accurate characterization given its predatory behavior toward small invertebrates and conspecifics. Family is listed as Grammidae by some retailers; FishBase's classification as Liopropomatidae is used as the authoritative source.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Liopropoma carmabi has been documented in at least one hobbyist record, making it one of the few Liopropoma spp. with a published captive-breeding account. However, successful captive breeding is considered rare and is generally regarded as advanced or not reliably reproducible in typical home aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for general husbandry baseline (water parameters, diet, tank size, reef-safe status). Temperature range (68–74°F) appears unusually cold versus other sources and wild FishBase data; averaged with other sources. Family listing (Grammidae) rejected in favor of FishBase Liopropomatidae.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Liopropomatidae, order Perciformes), maximum size (6.0 cm TL), depth range (15–70 m), native distribution, and wild temperature data (24.2–28°C mean 26.8°C). Cross-checked against LiveAquaria; temperature disagreement documented.

additional.name: Marine Collectors
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.5 in / 6.35 cm), temperature (72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C), pH (7.8–8.5), salinity (1.022–1.026), minimum tank size (10 gal), temperament (semi-aggressive), and native range (Western Atlantic, Curacao). Designated secondary Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; Marine Collectors substituted as a working cross-source. Family listed as Grammidae — rejected in favor of FishBase.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been documented in at least one hobbyist account (TCMAS Forums breeding journal) but is considered rare. Pairs reportedly spawn in typical serranid fashion; successfully raising larvae to juvenile stage is the main challenge.
